content:
# Genesis Chapter 33 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19r–19v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 30–31 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035

---

*Jacob Sees Esau Coming — He Arranges His Family (vv. 1–3)*

**33:1** And Jacob lifted up [וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב] his eyes [עֵינָיו] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] Esau [עֵשָׂו] was coming [בָּא] and with him [וְעִמּוֹ] four hundred men [אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת אִישׁ] — and he divided [וַיַּחַץ] the children [אֶת-הַיְלָדִים] unto Leah [עַל-לֵאָה] and unto Rachel [וְעַל-רָחֵל] and unto the two female-servants [וְעַל-שְׁתֵּי הַשְּׁפָחוֹת].

**33:2** And he put [וַיָּשֶׂם] the female-servants [אֶת-הַשְּׁפָחוֹת] and their children [וְאֶת-יַלְדֵיהֶן] first [רִאשֹׁנָה] — and Leah [וְאֶת-לֵאָה] and her children [וְאֶת-יְלָדֶיהָ] after [אַחֲרֹנִים] — and Rachel [וְאֶת-רָחֵל] and Joseph [וְאֶת-יוֹסֵף] last [אַחֲרֹנִים].

**33:3** And he himself [וְהוּא] passed over [עָבַר] before them [לִפְנֵיהֶם] — and he bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ] to the ground [אַרְצָה] seven times [שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים] — until he drew near [עַד-גִּשְׁתּוֹ] to his brother [עַד-אָחִיו].

---

*Esau Runs to Meet Jacob — The Reconciliation (v. 4)*

**33:4** And Esau ran [וַיָּרָץ עֵשָׂו] to meet him [לִקְרָאתוֹ] and he embraced him [וַיְחַבְּקֵהוּ] and he fell [וַיִּפֹּל] upon his neck [עַל-צַוָּארָו] and he kissed him [וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ] ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ — and they wept [וַיִּבְכּוּ].

---

*Jacob Introduces His Family to Esau (vv. 5–7)*

**33:5** And he lifted up [וַיִּשָּׂא] his eyes [עֵינָיו] and he saw [וַיַּרְא] the women [אֶת-הַנָּשִׁים] and the children [וְאֶת-הַיְלָדִים] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Who [מִי] are these [אֵלֶּה] to you [לָךְ]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: The children [הַיְלָדִים] that God [אֱלֹהִים] has graciously given [חָנַן] your servant [אֶת-עַבְדֶּךָ].

**33:6** And the female-servants drew near [וַתִּגַּשְׁןָ הַשְּׁפָחוֹת] — they [הֵנָּה] and their children [וְיַלְדֵיהֶן] — and they bowed down [וַתִּשְׁתַּחֲוֶיןָ].

**33:7** And Leah also [וַתִּגַּשׁ גַּם-לֵאָה] drew near [וַתִּגַּשׁ] and her children [וִילָדֶיהָ] — and they bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ] — and afterward [וְאַחַר] Joseph drew near [נִגַּשׁ יוֹסֵף] and Rachel [וְרָחֵל] — and they bowed down [וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ].

---

*The Question of the Gift-Caravan (vv. 8–11)*

**33:8** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: What [מִי] is to you [לְךָ] all this [כָּל-הַמַּחֲנֶה] camp [הַמַּחֲנֶה] that I met [אֲשֶׁר פָּגַשְׁתִּי]? And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: To find [לִמְצֹא] favor [חֵן] in the eyes of [בְּעֵינֵי] my lord [אֲדֹנִי].

**33:9** And Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו]: I have much [יֶשׁ-לִי רָב] — my brother [אָחִי] — let what is yours be yours [יְהִי לְךָ אֲשֶׁר-לָךְ].

**33:10** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב]: No — please [אַל-נָא] — if [אִם] I have found [מָצָאתִי] favor [חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֶיךָ] then receive [וְלָקַחְתָּ] my gift [מִנְחָתִי] from my hand [מִיָּדִי] — for [כִּי] therefore [עַל-כֵּן] I have seen [רָאִיתִי] your face [פָנֶיךָ] as one sees [כִּרְאֹת] the face of God [פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים] — and you were pleased with me [וַתִּרְצֵנִי].

**33:11** Take [קַח-נָא] — please — my blessing [אֶת-בִּרְכָתִי] that has been brought [אֲשֶׁר הֻבָאת] to you [לָךְ] — for God has favored me [כִּי-חַנַּנִי אֱלֹהִים] — and because [וְכִי] I have everything [יֶשׁ-לִי-כֹל] — and he urged him [וַיִּפְצַר-בּוֹ] — and he took [וַיִּקָּח].

---

*Esau Offers an Escort — Jacob Declines (vv. 12–15)*

**33:12** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Let us travel [נִסְעָה] and let us go [וְנֵלֵכָה] — and I will go [וְאֵלְכָה] before you [לְנֶגְדֶּךָ].

**33:13** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר] to him [אֵלָיו]: My lord [אֲדֹנִי] knows [יֹדֵעַ] that the children [כִּי הַיְלָדִים] are tender [רַכִּים] — and the flocks [וְהַצֹּאן] and the cattle [וְהַבָּקָר] that are giving-suck [עָלוֹת] are upon me [עָלַי] — and if they overdrive them [וּדְפָקוּם] one day [יוֹם אֶחָד] then all the flocks will die [וָמֵתוּ כָּל-הַצֹּאן].

**33:14** Let [יַעֲבָר-נָא] my lord [אֲדֹנִי] — please — pass over [יַעֲבָר] before [לִפְנֵי] his servant [עַבְדּוֹ] — and I myself [וַאֲנִי] will lead along gently [אֶתְנַהֲלָה לְאַטִּי] to the foot of [לְרֶגֶל] the work [הַמְּלָאכָה] that is before me [אֲשֶׁר לְפָנָי] and to the foot of [וּלְרֶגֶל] the children [הַיְלָדִים] — until I come [עַד אֲשֶׁר-אָבֹא] to my lord [אֶל-אֲדֹנִי] to Seir [שֵׂעִירָה].

**33:15** And Esau said [וַיֹּאמֶר עֵשָׂו]: Let me set [אַצִּיגָה-נָּא] — please — some of the people [מִן-הָעָם] that are with me [אֲשֶׁר אִתִּי] with you [עִמָּךְ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Why [לָמָּה] this [זֶה]? Let me find [אֶמְצָא-חֵן] favor [חֵן] in the eyes of [בְּעֵינֵי] my lord [אֲדֹנִי].

---

*The Parting of Ways — Esau Returns to Seir (v. 16)*

**33:16** And Esau returned [וַיָּשָׁב עֵשָׂו] on that day [בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא] on his way [לְדַרְכּוֹ] — to Seir [שֵׂעִירָה].

---

*Jacob Journeys to Succoth (v. 17)*

**33:17** And Jacob traveled [וְיַעֲקֹב נָסַע] to Succoth [סֻכֹּתָה] — and he built [וַיִּבֶן] for himself [לוֹ] a house [בַּיִת] — and for his livestock [וּלְמִקְנֵהוּ] he made [עָשָׂה] booths [סֻכֹּת] — therefore [עַל-כֵּן] he called [קָרָא] the name of [שֵׁם] the place [הַמָּקוֹם] Succoth [סֻכּוֹת].

---

*Jacob Arrives at Shechem — Buys Land — Erects an Altar (vv. 18–20)*

**33:18** And Jacob came [וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב] shalem [שָׁלֵם] — a city of Shechem [עִיר שְׁכֶם] that is in the land of Canaan [אֲשֶׁר בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן] — in his coming from [בְּבֹאוֹ מִ] Paddan-aram [פַּדַּן אֲרָם] — and he encamped [וַיִּחַן] before [אֶת-פְּנֵי] the city [הָעִיר].

**33:19** And he bought [וַיִּקֶן] the portion of the field [אֶת-חֶלְקַת הַשָּׂדֶה] where he had spread [אֲשֶׁר נָטָה-שָׁם] his tent [אָהֳלוֹ] — from the hand of [מִיַּד] the sons of Hamor [בְּנֵי-חֲמוֹר] the father of [אֲבִי] Shechem [שְׁכֶם] — for a hundred qesitah [בְּמֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה].

**33:20** And he set up [וַיַּצֶּב-שָׁם] there [שָׁם] a pillar-altar [מִזְבֵּחַ] — and he called it [וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ] El-Elohe-Israel [אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 19r–19v:**

LC page 1 (GEN_033_page_001.jpg) is folio 19r of the Leningrad Codex, photographed in color by the West Semitic Research Project. The three-column Sephardic square script is fully vocalized with Tiberian pointing and cantillation marks throughout. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 32 (the sinew prohibition, ch. 32:32–33) in the rightmost column, with the parasha marker visible at the chapter break. Genesis 33 opens in the middle column and continues through the left column, reading from right to left across the page in the standard Hebrew direction. I can read clearly on this folio the chapter opening (וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב עֵינָיו, v. 1), the family arrangement, the reconciliation pericope (vv. 4–7), the gift dialogue (vv. 8–11), and Esau's offer of an escort (vv. 12–15). The cantillation marks are clear throughout; the vocalization (Tiberian niqqud) is present on all words. The name יְהוָה (YHWH) does not appear in Genesis 33 — the divine name in this chapter is אֱלֹהִים (*Elohim*) throughout.

LC page 2 (GEN_033_page_002.jpg) is folio 19v. It carries the conclusion of Genesis 33 (vv. 16–20) and the opening verses of Genesis 34. The three-column layout is clearly legible. Key readings confirmed on this folio: the Succoth naming (סֻכּוֹת, v. 17) with the etiological note; שָׁלֵם (v. 18) with its ambiguous pointing; the land purchase at a hundred קְשִׂיטָה (qesitah, v. 19); and the altar inscription אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (v. 20). The parasha break that opens Genesis 34 is visible at the bottom of this folio.

**On reading Codex Vaticanus, Pages 30–31:**

Codex Vaticanus page 30 (GEN_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the heading CAP. XXXVII at the top center, which is the LXX chapter numbering for the Genesis 33 material (as discussed in the GEN_032 notes, the LXX chapter numbering is offset from the MT). The continuous uncial Greek script, without word-breaks or diacritical marks, is legible across three columns. The page carries the LXX text of Genesis 33 from its opening through approximately v. 15, reading in sequence with the MT. Page 31 (GEN_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) has CAP. XXXVIII visible in the right margin, corresponding to the opening of Genesis 34 in the LXX chapter system, and carries the conclusion of Genesis 33 (LXX vv. 16–20) before transitioning to ch. 34.

---

**33:1 — "And Jacob lifted up his eyes and saw" (*wayyissa' Ya'aqov 'einaw wayyar'*, וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא):**
The phrase נָשָׂא עֵינַיִם (*nasa' 'einayim*, "to lift up the eyes") is a recurring narrative idiom in Genesis for a character turning attention to something unexpected or newly visible (cf. 13:10, 14; 18:2; 22:4, 13; 24:63–64; 31:10). It signals the opening of a scene and often precedes a consequential sighting. Jacob "lifts his eyes" here in direct echo of the lifting of eyes in 22:4 (Abraham sees Moriah) and 22:13 (Abraham sees the ram) — a narrative pattern that links moments of decisive encounter. The same phrase will appear again in v. 5 when Esau lifts his eyes to see Jacob's family. EMET translates the idiom literally throughout. The LXX Vaticanus reads *anablepsas de Iakōb tois ophthalmois* (ἀναβλέψας δε Ἰακώβ τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς) — "and Jacob having looked up with [his] eyes" — using *anablepō* (to look up/lift the eyes) in a participial construction. The Greek is an exact semantic equivalent.

---

**33:1 — Division of the Family (*wayyachats*, וַיַּחַץ) — Tactical Ordering:**
The verb חָצָה (*chatsah*, "to divide, to halve, to split") is the same root used in 32:7 when Jacob divided his camp into two for tactical reasons before the wrestling encounter. Here the division is not military but domestic — and yet the military logic is still present: Jacob positions the less beloved (the female-servants and their children) at the front, where danger would strike first, and his most beloved (Rachel and Joseph) at the rear. The ordering inverts the usual Hebrew narrative sequence of honor: here exposure to danger is correlated with lower status. The arrangement — female-servants first, then Leah and her children, then Rachel and Joseph last — is the reverse of Jacob's emotional priority (Rachel first, Joseph first) but the forward deployment of those he cares for least. This is one of the most coldly calculated acts in the Jacob narrative, and EMET reports it without softening. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai emerisen* (καὶ ἐμέρισεν) — "and he divided" — the verb *merizō* preserving the same sense.

---

**33:3 — Seven Prostrations (*sheva' pe'amim*, שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים):**
The act of seven prostrations before Esau — Jacob bowing face to the ground seven times as he approaches — reflects the full ancient Near Eastern protocol of submission before a superior. The number seven (*sheva'*) is the complete number in the Hebrew symbolic system and here signifies total, unreserved obeisance. Remarkably, archaeological parallels are exact: the Amarna letters (14th century BCE diplomatic correspondence from Canaan to the Egyptian Pharaoh) contain repeated phrases such as "seven times and seven times I bow, upon the belly and upon the back" — the same posture and the same seven-fold repetition as a formula of diplomatic submission to a great lord. Jacob — who had taken the birthright and the blessing, who was prophesied to be lord over his brother — performs the vassal's prostration before the brother he had supplanted. The irony is structural: the blessing of Isaac promised that peoples would bow to Jacob (27:29); Jacob bows seven times to Esau. The LXX Vaticanus reads *prosekynesesen epi tēn gēn heptakis* (προσεκύνησεν ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ἑπτάκις) — "he did obeisance upon the ground seven times."

---

**33:4 — The Dots Above vayyishshaqehu (וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ) — Puncta Extraordinaria:**
The word וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ (*vayyishshaqehu*, "and he kissed him") bears in the Masoretic Text a series of raised dots (*puncta extraordinaria*, נְקֻדּוֹת, *neqüddot*) above each of the word's letters. On the LC manuscript page, these dots are visible to the trained eye of the scholar — small supralinear marks placed above the consonants of וישקהו, a scribal annotation preserved with scrupulous care through the centuries. Puncta extraordinaria appear in fifteen passages in the Masoretic tradition (the Babylonian Talmud, tractate Sofrim 6:3, and later Masoretic lists catalog them). Their precise significance is debated, but their effect is to signal to the reader: *pay attention; there is something unusual here; this word invites special scrutiny*.

EMET marks the puncta in the translation text with the symbol ˙˙˙˙˙˙˙ following the translated word, to make the scribal signal visible to the reader.

The rabbinic interpretive tradition (e.g., *Bereshit Rabbah* 78:9; *Sifre* Numbers §69) offers multiple readings of this scribal signal:
1. **The kiss was not genuine** — Esau approached to bite (*lishokh*) Jacob on the neck rather than kiss (*lishaq*) him, but the dots mark the word as suspicious; Jacob's neck miraculously hardened like marble, thwarting Esau's attempt. The two words נשק (to kiss) and נשך (to bite) are easily confused in rapid speech. This reading makes Esau the aggressor still.
2. **The kiss was genuine** — The dots mark the verse as surprising or counter to expectation: *despite everything*, Esau embraced and kissed Jacob with sincere tears. The reconciliation is real. The dots flag this as extraordinary, almost incredible — that Esau could truly embrace the brother who stole his blessing.
3. **The dots are a textual/scribal uncertainty marker** — the original scribe was uncertain whether this word was in the original text or was a later addition, and marked it for future scholars to evaluate.

EMET declines to adjudicate among these readings in the translation itself. The dots are here, in the manuscript, above this word. They are translated as they stand: the text says "and he kissed him," marked with scribal uncertainty or emphasis. The reconciliation is textually present and textually scrutinized simultaneously. The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai ephilēsen auton* (καὶ ἐφίλησεν αὐτόν) — "and he kissed/befriended him" — the verb *phileō* (to love with affection, to kiss) with no hesitation. The LXX has no equivalent to the puncta; it reads the reconciliation without reservation.

---

**33:5 — "God has graciously given" (*channan Elohim*, חָנַן אֱלֹהִים):**
The verb חָנַן (*chanan*, "to be gracious, to show grace, to give as a gift") is the verbal root underlying the noun חֵן (*chen*, "grace, favor") and the name חַנָּה (*Channah*, "Hannah"). Jacob calls his children a gift of divine grace — the same divine grace (חֵן) he had sought from Esau in v. 8. The circle of grace (seeking favor from Esau; acknowledging God's favor in the children) is deliberately constructed by the narrator. Jacob, who had grasped and maneuvered throughout his life, here uses the language of gift and gratuity. EMET translates *channan* as "has graciously given" — the perfect of *chanan* with the object implied — rather than "has been gracious," to preserve the transitive force of the verb (God gave the children as acts of grace). The LXX Vaticanus reads *ēleēsen ho Theos* (ἠλέησεν ὁ Θεός) — "God had mercy/showed mercy" — using *eleeō* (to have compassion, to show mercy) rather than a grace/gift word, shifting the register slightly from gracious-giving to compassionate-mercy.

---

**33:10 — "As one sees the face of God" (*kir'ot penei Elohim*, כִּרְאֹת פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים) — The Peniel Echo:**
This verse is one of the most theologically freighted statements in Genesis 33 and must be read in direct light of Genesis 32:30. At Peniel, Jacob had declared: *ra'iti Elohim panim el-panim* — "I have seen God face to face" (32:30). Now Jacob says to Esau: "I have seen your face [*ra'iti fanekha*] as one sees the face of God [*kir'ot penei Elohim*]." The parallel is unmistakable in the Hebrew and on the manuscript page: the face-seeing language (*ra'ah panim*) that was used for the divine encounter at Peniel is now applied to the human encounter with Esau.

This is not merely metaphor or flattery. The narrative structure asserts something profound: Jacob has seen God's face and survived (32:30); now he faces Esau — who came with 400 men, who was the object of Jacob's terror and prayer — and he has survived that too. To see Esau's face welcoming him is, for Jacob, equivalent in grace and weight to seeing the face of God. The encounter with the brother who could have destroyed him is treated as a theophany of its own kind.

The phrase *wattirtseni* (וַתִּרְצֵנִי, "and you were pleased with me / and you accepted me") adds the dimension of acceptance — Jacob passed through the gauntlet of divine encounter and human encounter both, and was accepted. EMET renders this literally without theological interpretation: the face-seeing comparison stands in the translation as it stands in the text. The LXX Vaticanus reads *hōs ei tis ophtai prosōpon Theou* (ὡς εἴ τις ὅπται πρόσωπον Θεοῦ) — "as if one beholds the face of God" — an exact rendering.

---

**33:11 — "My blessing" (*birkhati*, בִּרְכָתִי) — The Stolen Blessing Returned:**
Jacob uses the word בִּרְכָתִי (*birkhati*, "my blessing") for the gift. The word בְּרָכָה (*berakha*, "blessing") is the same word used for the blessing Jacob stole from Esau in Genesis 27: *birkhat yitschaq* (the blessing of Isaac, 27:41), *birkhatekha* (your blessing, 27:36). Jacob had taken Esau's blessing (*berakha*) from Isaac; now he offers Esau his own blessing (*birkhati*) in the form of the gift-caravan. Whether this is intended as restitution, irony, or simple idiom (a gift presented as a blessing upon the recipient) is left for the reader to determine. EMET translates *birkhati* literally as "my blessing" and marks the interpretive possibility in this note. The LXX Vaticanus reads *tas eulogias mou* (τὰς εὐλογίας μου) — "my blessings" — *eulogia* being the standard LXX translation for *berakha* throughout the Jacob-Esau narrative.

---

**33:12–15 — "I will lead along gently to the foot of the work" (*etnahăla le'atti le-regel ha-mela'khah*, אֶתְנַהֲלָה לְאַטִּי לְרֶגֶל הַמְּלָאכָה):**
The verb אֶתְנַהֲלָה (*etnahăla*) is the Hithpa'el imperfect first person singular of נָהַל (*nahal*, "to lead, to conduct, to guide [as a shepherd leading a flock to water]"). The Hithpa'el reflexive-iterative stem here means "I will lead myself / I will proceed at my own pace / I will travel gently along." The idiom לְאַטִּי (*le'atti*) — "at my gentleness / at my slowness / gently" — is from אַט (*at*, "gently, slowly, carefully"). Together the phrase is: "I will guide [myself and the flock] gently, at [our] own pace." The expression לְרֶגֶל (*le-regel*, "to the foot/pace of") + the object (the work / the children) means "at the pace that the work [i.e., the livestock and children] can maintain." EMET renders this with careful literalism: "I will lead along gently to the foot of the work that is before me and to the foot of the children." The idiom is awkward in English, but the awkwardness is in the text. The pastoral realism of the verse — a man who genuinely cannot travel quickly with nursing animals and small children — gives Jacob's refusal of Esau's escort a completely human, non-political texture. The LXX Vaticanus reads *ego katastesomai en tachei* (ἐγὼ καταστήσομαι ἐν τάχει) — "I will move along quickly" — which is, remarkably, the opposite of the MT's *le'atti* ("gently/slowly"). The LXX appears to have read or rendered the verb differently; the MT is preferable and coherent with the pastoral context. This is a notable LXX variant.

---

**33:16 — Esau Returns to Seir — Jacob Does Not Follow:**
Jacob's promise to come to Esau at Seir (v. 14 — *'ad asher-avo el-adoni se'irah*, "until I come to my lord to Seir") is never fulfilled in the narrative. Esau departs for Seir; Jacob travels to Succoth and then to Shechem — westward and northward, not south to Seir. Whether this is deliberate evasion, a change of plans, or simply the narrator's way of recording the permanent separation of the two peoples (Israel and Edom), the text is silent. EMET does not interpolate a resolution. The separation is the end of the story. The two brothers have reconciled — and parted.

---

**33:17 — Succoth (*Sukkot*, סֻכּוֹת) — "Booths":**
The place-name סֻכּוֹת (*Sukkot*) means "booths, shelters, huts, tabernacles" — from the root סָכַךְ (*sakhakh*, "to cover, to make a covering") via the noun סֻכָּה (*sukkah*, "booth, shelter"). The etiological note is explicit: Jacob built booths (סֻכֹּת) for his livestock, therefore the place was called Booths (סֻכּוֹת). The word *sukkah* is the same word used for the Festival of Booths (*Chag ha-Sukkot*, Leviticus 23:33–43), the autumn harvest festival during which Israel dwells in booths to commemorate the wilderness wandering. The place-name Succoth was historically identifiable east of the Jordan (cf. 1 Kings 7:46; Psalm 60:6 [MT 60:8]; Judges 8:4–16 — Gideon's conflict at Succoth). Jacob builds a house for himself and booths for his livestock — his first act of settlement after crossing back into the land is to build: he has arrived. The LXX Vaticanus reads *Skēnas* (Σκηνάς) — "Tents / Tabernacles" — *skēnē* being the standard LXX word for the booth/tabernacle, the same word used throughout the Pentateuch for the Tabernacle (*mishkan*).

---

**33:18 — shalem (*shalem*, שָׁלֵם) — Ambiguity: Adverb or Proper Noun?**
The word שָׁלֵם is one of the most contested readings in Genesis 33. It stands immediately after the verb *wayyavo* (and he came) before "a city of Shechem." Two readings are possible:

1. **Adverb / adjective** — *shalem* from שָׁלַם (*shalem*, "to be whole, complete, unharmed, at peace") — "And Jacob came *safely/in peace/whole* to the city of Shechem." Under this reading, *shalem* is a narrative comment on Jacob's condition: he arrived unharmed (the fulfillment of his vow at Bethel in 28:21, *we-shavti be-shalom*, "and I return in peace to my father's house"). The NJPS, NRSV, and many modern translations prefer this reading.

2. **Proper noun / toponym** — *Shalem* as the name of a city or district — "And Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem." Under this reading, Shalem is a place-name (perhaps identical with the ancient Jerusalem/Salem of Genesis 14:18, or a separate locality in the Shechem region). The older tradition (Vulgate: *Hierusalem*) and some rabbinic readings treat this as a toponym.

The Masoretic Text presents שָׁלֵם without an attached *he* locale (שָׁלֵמָה, which would clearly mark directionality to a place-name) and without the definite article (הַשָּׁלֵם, which might mark it as a definite adjective). The word stands alone, unpunctuated by grammatical markers that would resolve the ambiguity. The context — the verse says "a city of Shechem" immediately after — suggests that if *shalem* were a full proper noun, the verse would be identifying two different places (Shalem and [the city of] Shechem), which is geographically redundant. More likely *shalem* modifies Jacob's arrival: he came *unharmed* to the city-district of Shechem.

EMET transliterates *shalem* in the translation text without resolution, marking both readings in this note. The reader must hold the ambiguity. The LXX Vaticanus reads *eis Salēm polin Sikimōn* (εἰς Σαλὴμ πόλιν Σικίμων) — "into Shalem, a city of the Shechemites" — treating it as a proper noun (Salēm). This is the LXX's most explicit endorsement of the toponym reading.

---

**33:19 — qesitah (*qesitah*, קְשִׂיטָה) — An Ancient and Rare Unit of Value:**
The word קְשִׂיטָה (*qesitah*, plural *qesitot*) is an ancient monetary or value-unit appearing only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible: here (Genesis 33:19), Job 42:11, and Joshua 24:32 (where the identical land-purchase at Shechem is cited in Joshua's farewell speech, confirming the tradition's deep memory of this transaction). The etymology of *qesitah* is uncertain. Proposals include:
- From an Akkadian root meaning "a weighed [amount]" — a weight-unit of silver
- From an Arabic cognate meaning "portion, share"
- An archaic Semitic monetary term that had fallen out of use by the monarchic period (explaining its extreme rarity in the text)

The ancient translators struggled with it: the LXX Vaticanus renders it *amnōn* (ἀμνῶν) — "of lambs" — treating *qesitah* as a lamb-unit (payment in sheep). The Vulgate renders it *centum agnis* — "a hundred lambs." The Targums and the Peshitta similarly translate it as a unit of sheep or coin. The meaning "lamb" for *qesitah* in the ancient versions likely reflects a tradition that the original payment was in livestock (which would be consistent with Jacob's wealth in animals), or an attempt to translate an obscure term by reference to a known unit. Modern lexicography (BDB, HALOT) treats *qesitah* as a monetary unit of unknown value, probably a weight-measure of silver.

EMET transliterates the term as "qesitah" and preserves the number hundred (*me'ah*, מֵאָה) exactly as stated. The literal unit is untranslatable with certainty; "a hundred qesitah" reports what the text says. This is one of the few cases where EMET's literalism produces a technical term that cannot be rendered in English — the bracket with a note is the honest scholar's response.

---

**33:20 — El-Elohe-Israel (*El Elohei Yisra'el*, אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל) — The First Use of Jacob's New Name in a Divine Title:**
The inscription Jacob gives to the altar is אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל — which means: "God, the God of Israel." This is the first time in the narrative that the name יִשְׂרָאֵל (*Yisra'el*, Israel) — given to Jacob at Peniel (32:28) — is used as a self-reference by Jacob in a theological formula. At Peniel, the name was given by the divine wrestler; here, Jacob takes ownership of it by inscribing it into the altar's name. The formula has three terms:

1. **אֵל** (*El*) — the generic Semitic word for "God" / "the divine being" — the most ancient designation
2. **אֱלֹהִים** in construct (*Elohei*) — "God of" — the standard plural-of-majesty divine name in its construct form, meaning "the God who belongs to" or "the God who is over"
3. **יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*Yisra'el*, Israel) — Jacob's new covenant name

The full phrase: "El, the God of Israel" — God [who is] the God of Israel. By erecting this altar and naming it thus, Jacob does three things simultaneously: he worships, he establishes a sacred site at Shechem, and he declares his new identity. The altar at Shechem echoes the earlier altars Abraham built there (12:6–7 — the first altar in Canaan, built under the terebinth of Moreh) and continues the line of patriarchal altar-building that marks the covenant history in the land.

The verb used for Jacob's action — וַיַּצֶּב (*vayyatsev*, "and he set up / erected") — is the same root used in 28:18 (*vayyatsev matsevah*, "and he set up a pillar") at Bethel. EMET renders this as "he set up... a pillar-altar," noting that *mizbēach* (מִזְבֵּחַ) is specifically an altar for sacrifice (from זָבַח, *zavach*, "to slaughter, to sacrifice"), not merely a standing stone. The distinction from *matsevah* (a commemorative pillar) is clear in the Hebrew; Jacob builds an altar of sacrifice here, not merely a memorial stone.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai estēsen ekei thysiastērion kai epekalesato ton Theon Israēl* (καὶ ἔστησεν ἐκεῖ θυσιαστήριον καὶ ἐπεκαλέσατο τὸν Θεὸν Ἰσραήλ) — "and he set up there an altar and called upon the God of Israel" — reorganizing the syntax so that Jacob *calls upon* (invokes in prayer) the God of Israel rather than inscribing a name on the altar. The MT construction (וַיִּקְרָא-לוֹ אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל, "and he called it El-Elohe-Israel") treats the name *El-Elohe-Israel* as the altar's name, which Jacob gives it. The LXX shifts the action from naming to invoking. EMET follows the MT.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 30–31):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 30–31 carry Genesis 33 in continuous Greek uncial script without word-breaks or accent marks, in three columns per page. Page 30 has the chapter heading CAP. XXXVII at the top center; page 31 shows CAP. XXXVIII beginning in the right-margin notation corresponding to the opening of LXX Genesis 34. The LXX chapter divisions confirm the offset noted in the GEN_032 notes.

Key LXX variants for Genesis 33:

1. **33:4 — No puncta** — The LXX reads *ephilēsen auton* (ἐφίλησεν αὐτόν) without any indication of the textual hesitation marked by the puncta extraordinaria in the MT. The LXX presents the kiss as straightforward and unambiguous.

2. **33:5 — "God had mercy"** — LXX *ēleēsen ho Theos* (ἠλέησεν ὁ Θεός) for MT *channan Elohim* ("God has graciously given"). LXX uses mercy/compassion (*eleeō*) where MT uses grace/gift (*chanan*). A slight semantic shift.

3. **33:10 — "As if one beholds the face of God"** — LXX *hōs ei tis ophtai prosōpon Theou* — exact rendering, preserving the Peniel echo.

4. **33:11 — "My blessings"** (plural *eulogias*) — for MT singular *birkhati* ("my blessing"). A minor variant with potential theological significance — the LXX pluralizes the gift-as-blessing.

5. **33:14 — "I will move along quickly" (*en tachei*)** — for MT *le'atti* ("gently/slowly"). A significant reversal; the LXX has Jacob claim he will travel quickly rather than slowly. This is the most notable LXX variant in this chapter, suggesting either a different Vorlage or a misreading of the Hebrew idiom.

6. **33:18 — "Into Shalem, a city of the Shechemites"** — LXX treats *shalem* as a proper noun (Σαλήμ) where the MT's *shalem* is ambiguous (adverb "safely" or toponym "Shalem"). The LXX reading favors the toponym interpretation.

7. **33:19 — "Lambs" (*amnōn*)** — LXX translates *qesitah* as *amnōn* (lambs), treating the ancient monetary term as a unit of livestock. This may reflect an ancient tradition or an attempt to render an opaque term.

8. **33:20 — "Called upon the God of Israel"** — LXX reorganizes from altar-naming (*wayyiqra'-lo El Elohei Yisra'el* = "he called it El-Elohe-Israel") to prayer-invocation (*epekalesato ton Theon Israēl* = "he called upon the God of Israel"). The MT names the altar; the LXX has Jacob pray.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 33 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness) are the two principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
